body:
before we introduce this query we should consider adding an index so that this can be performed efficiently. We likely want `createdByUserId, type = recent_search, updated_at desc`

we might want to add an index on `type, updated_at` as well so that we can efficiently filter out this type from the fetchSearchEntities semantic search job:

+#!/usr/bin/env sh
+
+# expects to be called with the full eslint command and its arguments
+# handles backing up and restoring suppressions around the eslint run
+# for a set of rules which are disabled runs with ESLINT_NO_TYPE_CHECKED=1
+
+# Forward signals to subcommand process so Ctrl-C correctly terminates it
+run_with_signal_forwarding() {
+	trap 'kill -TERM $CMD_PID 2>/dev/null' TERM INT HUP QUIT
+	"$@" &
+	CMD_PID=$!
+	wait $CMD_PID
+	CMD_EXIT_CODE=$?
+	trap - TERM INT HUP QUIT
+	return $CMD_EXIT_CODE
+}
+
+# Run ESLint with backup/restore only if ESLINT_NO_TYPE_CHECKED is set
+
+# back up suppressions file before running ESLint

pr_title: feat(api): add /public/v0/tables/query endpoint
body:
@claude please give this pr a tough but fair review. Pay special attention to the standards for the public api that are outlined here:

# Public API — Response Envelope Conventions

This is the public, customer-facing API surface. Every new endpoint must fit one
of the response shapes documented here. Once the API is in customer hands,
**renaming or removing a response field is breaking; only adding new optional**
**fields is safe**. Get the shape right before the endpoint ships.

Contracts live in `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/`. Endpoint handlers live
under `apps/api/public-api/v0/`.

## Errors

All non-2xx responses use:

```ts
{ message: string }
```

Defined as `errorResponseSchema` in `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/common.ts`.
HTTP status is the only error discriminator today. Do not add ad-hoc fields
(`success`, `code`, `error`, `details`, …) to error bodies — a structured error
envelope (with stable machine-readable codes) is planned and will be added in
one place when it lands.

## Single resource

Successful reads and creates return the resource as a flat object with its ID at
the root.

```ts
{ id: string, ... } // generic resource — see PublicToolSummary
{ searchId: string, ... } // domain-specific id when it reads better
```

## Collections (cursor pagination)

For lists where pages are stable and re-fetchable:

```ts
{ data: T[], cursor?: string }
```

Use `paginatedQuerySchema` + `paginatedResponse` from
`libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/pagination.ts` (default limit 20, max 100).
Omit `cursor` from the response when there are no more pages.

Pagination intentionally omits a `total` count today — many endpoints can't
compute it cheaply, and adding it later is additive. If you genuinely need it
for a specific endpoint, raise it as a convention change rather than adding it
ad-hoc on one endpoint.

## Iterators (stateful streams)

For endpoints that consume state on each call and cannot offer stable
re-fetching of a previous page (e.g., `searches/:id/next`):

```ts
{ data: T[], hasMore: boolean }
```

Don't expose a `cursor` here — it would imply re-fetchability the iterator
doesn't support. Pagination input (`limit`, etc.) goes in the request body.

## Async jobs

Endpoints that kick off background work and are polled for results follow a
`status`\-discriminated shape:

```ts
// 202 from the kick-off endpoint
{ <resourceId>, status: 'in_progress' }

// 202 from the polling endpoint while still running
{ <resourceId>, status: 'in_progress', total: number, finished: number }

// 200 from the polling endpoint once terminal
{ <resourceId>, status: 'complete' | …, total, finished, … }
```

The kickoff response is intentionally bare. The caller just sent the request and
already knows what they submitted, so echoing counts back is dead weight. The
polling response carries `total` and `finished` so clients can render progress
(`${finished}/${total}`) without threading their input size through their own
state.

HTTP status mirrors lifecycle: **202 while in progress, 200 once terminal**. The
`status` literal is kept on the 202 response too so clients can branch on
`status` alone without also keying off HTTP status.

Terminal payload varies by transport: small/inline results use `data: T[]` plus
optional `cursor` for pagination; large/file results use a `resultUrl` string
(e.g. a presigned URL). Pick whichever fits the result size — both are accepted
under this envelope.

Use the same `<resourceId>` field name across kickoff, polling, and any sibling
endpoints in the domain — don't ship `toolRunId` on one and `runId` on another.

Reference shapes: `RunToolResponseSchema`, `RunResultsInProgressSchema`,
`RunResultsCompleteSchema` in `tools-contract.ts`.

## Request shape conventions

- **Resource identifiers go in the path, not the query string.** Use
`GET /tools/run/:toolRunId/results`, not `GET /tools/run/results?toolRunId=…`.
Query parameters are for modifiers (filters, pagination, options) that don't
identify the resource being addressed.
- **No empty-body POSTs.** If a POST has no input, declare `body: c.noBody()`.
Don't require `body: z.object({})` — many HTTP clients won't send `{}` by
default and customers will hit it as a friction point.

## Field-naming conventions

- Async-job counts: **`total`** **and** **`finished`** — never `totalItems`,
`itemCount`, `completed`, `numDone`, etc.
- IDs at the top level: use a domain-specific name when one reads naturally
(`toolRunId`, `searchId`); use plain `id` for first-class resources
(`PublicToolSummary.id`). Pick one name per concept and reuse it across all
endpoints in that domain — don't ship `toolRunId` on one endpoint and `runId`
on a sibling.
- Lowercase snake-style enum literals: `'in_progress'`, `'complete'`,
`'validation_failed'`. Match what already exists in the contract before
inventing a new vocabulary.

## Evolution rules

| Change | Safe? |
| --- | --- |
| Add a new endpoint | ✅ Safe |
| Add a new optional response field | ✅ Safe |
| Add a new `status` value to an async-job union | ⚠️ Safe only if clients are documented to handle unknown statuses; otherwise treat as breaking |
| Remove a field | ❌ Breaking |
| Rename a field | ❌ Breaking |
| Make a required response field optional | ❌ Breaking |
| Make an optional response field required | ⚠️ Safe for the server (it always sends it now) but clients that defaulted on absence may regress; treat as breaking unless reviewed |
| Change a literal value | ❌ Breaking |
| Change which HTTP status carries which state | ❌ Breaking |

Subtractive changes after launch require versioning the API path
(`/public/v1/...`) or shipping the new shape alongside the old.

## Where the building blocks live

| Concern | File |
| --- | --- |
| Error envelope | `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/common.ts` |
| Pagination helpers | `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/pagination.ts` |
| Async-job reference | `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/tools-contract.ts` |
| Iterator reference | `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/searches-contract.ts` |
| OpenAPI generation | `libs/api-contract/src/public-api-v0/openapi.ts` (regenerate via `yarn workspace @org/api-contract generate:public-api-openapi` after any contract change) |
| Gate / auth middleware | `apps/api/public-api/gate.ts` |
| Rate limiting | `apps/api/public-api/rate-limit.ts` |

if I understand what budget id is, I don't think it makes sense to live here or on access_tokens

oauth authorization codes are short lived (5 min TTL) codes that can be used once to issue an access token. the access token is then used to actually authenticate api requests to our server, and also is short lived with a 1 hour TTL.

see here for more details on the auth code grant flow if you're interested:

Furthermore, I imagine we want users to be able to modify this outside of just the oauth consent flow that they may only trigger every few months before tokens are refreshed automatically in the background.

seems more appropriate that this lives on the user workspace relationship, or if we want per oauth client configurability then we likely need to create a new entity like `oauth_grants` or something to that effect

Just want to check my understanding here, is the plan that we might ahve duplicate tools corresponding to different app account id's? What makes us expect users will need this?

the way I imagined this would work is that a given enrichment would only be represented by a single tool, and you need to select a default app account for it. Then if you change the app account, it changes what is used across all invocations of that tool.

I can't think of a reason a user would want two separate instance of the same tool both being used, but pulling from different app accounts, but I may have a poor understanding of user behavior in this domain.

also it would probably make more sense to just have `isDataPoint` that asserts `is DataPoint` but doesn't make any assertions on the `state`, and then the caller is responsible for narrowing down the state. If there are many callers that care about this specific `in-progress` or `pending` states we should define new types in a discriminated union on `DataPoint` like `InProgressDataPoint` and `PendingDataPoint` that we could then further asser the type down to with something like:

```
function isPendingDataPoint(dp: unknown): dp is PendingDataPoint {
 return isDataPoint(dp) && dp.state === 'Pending'
}
```
